name: Hastamalaka
dates_notes: Known only from sectarian tradition as one of Śaṅkara's four chief disciples; one short hymn survives.
core_thesis: Hastāmalaka is known almost exclusively through one short *stotra*. The position articulated there is *nirupādhika-ātmā-jñāna*: the *ātman* is the eternal self-luminous (*svayam-prakāśa*) witness-consciousness (*sākṣin*), identical with *brahman*, intrinsically free of the body-mind aggregate's transformations. The *aham*-cognition refers to this *ātman* when understood at its *paramārtha* depth — not at the level of *adhyāsa*, where *aham* attaches to body, *prāṇa*, or mental states. The hymn's refrain — *nityopalabdhi-svarūpo 'ham ātmā*, "I am the self whose nature is constant cognizance" — is a compact statement of the Advaita identity-thesis from the first-person *anubhava*-side. Beyond what the hymn says, the historical evidence is essentially nil. The *Maṭha* tradition treats Hastāmalaka as the founder of one of the four cardinal *āmnāya* lineages, but no biographical or doctrinal corpus survives. The figure must therefore be engaged as a name-and-hymn, not as a developed authorial position; the doctrinal content is the content of the hymn itself.

engaged_works.title: Hastamalaka-Stotra
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed; the hymn is universally received in the Advaita maṭha tradition though independent academic verification is limited.
engaged_works.summary: A short *stotra* of twelve verses; some recensions add a *phala-śruti*. Tradition frames the hymn with a narrative: the boy Hastāmalaka, asked by Śaṅkara *ko bhavān* ("who are you?"), responds in spontaneous verse, identifying himself not with the body-mind but with the witness-consciousness *ātman*. Each verse follows one structural pattern. It begins by describing mental and bodily activities — the operations of *manas*, *buddhi*, *indriyas*, the alternation of *jāgrat*, *svapna*, *suṣupti*, the cycle of *karma* — and closes with a refrain affirming the speaker's identity with the unchanging self-luminous *ātman* who illuminates all of these without being any of them. The hymn is recited liturgically in Advaita *maṭha* contexts and stands in the standard *prakaraṇa* corpus alongside short Śaṅkara-ascribed *stotras*. Ascription is traditional. The hymn is universally received in the Advaita *maṭha* tradition; independent academic verification is limited, and the historical Hastāmalaka cannot be securely identified beyond the tradition's claim.
